chunk_summary: This section outlines red flags and referral triggers for stunting in children, emphasizing a structured approach to identify underlying diseases and prevent mismanagement.
learning_objectives: Understand the significance of stunting (HAZ < -2 SD) as a phenotype requiring etiologic evaluation.
Identify systemic red flags and their implications for underlying diseases in stunted children.
Learn to differentiate between normal variants and pathological causes of short stature using familial height tracking and clinical patterns.
Recognize the urgency of coexisting stunting and wasting (WaSt phenotype) or acute illness.
key_facts: Stunting is defined as height-for-age Z-score (HAZ) < -2 SD.
A child tracking within the familial height range is more likely a normal variant.
Red flags include disproportionate short stature, dysmorphism, microcephaly/macrocephaly, chronic diarrhea, recurrent infections, and endocrine symptoms like pubertal delay.
Coexisting stunting and wasting (WaSt phenotype) or acute illness increases clinical urgency.
Clinical notes should explicitly state either 'stunting without red flags' or 'stunting with red flags' to guide management.
reference_quote: A child who is short but proportionate, developing normally, and tracking consistently within the expected familial band is more likely a normal variant; a child who is far below the familial band or crossing down height centiles needs evaluation. (Source: IAP Growth Monitoring Guidelines)
document_key_topics: Red flags for underlying diseases in stunted children
Differentiating normal variants from pathological short stature
Urgency in cases of coexisting stunting and wasting
